test_id_1.query_content: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
2

Pharmacy Providers
Dual Eligible Coverage
For NYS Medicaid members who are enrolled in a Medicare Part D drug plan or Medicare Advantage Drug
Contracting (MAPD) plan, NYRx provides prescription drug coverage only for the drugs listed below. To
view all drugs covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible NYS Medicaid members, providers can refer to the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) “Drugs Covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible Members Effective 10/22/2020” web
page, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/medicare
_exempt_drugs.htm. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan.
For the most up to date coverage information for the products below, providers should refer to the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/fullform.pdf.
Prescription Drugs:
• Vitamins
Folic acid – oral
Hydroxocobalamin – injection
Vitamin B12 – injection/nasal
Vitamin D2 – oral
Vitamin K – oral
• Cough and Cold
• Benzonatate
Please note: NYRx does not provide dual eligible members with coverage of compounded
prescriptions.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For dual eligible pharmacy claims, the current provider manual says Medicare Part D is generally primary and Medicaid does not pay for Part D-covered drugs for full-benefit dual eligible members. The policy update matches that result and says other prescription medications should be billed to the Part D plan first.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare and Medicaid dual eligible members, Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must use a Medicare Part D plan and the plans exception and appeals process for nonformulary needs.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For NYS Medicaid members enrolled in Medicare Part D or MAPD, NYRx covers only a limited list of drugs. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan. The policy is broader because it also points providers to a list of covered dual eligible drugs.

similarities: Both sources say Medicare Part D is the primary billing path for dual eligible pharmacy claims when the drug is Part D covered [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both sources limit Medicaid or NYRx coverage to a narrower set of drugs for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about full-benefit dual eligible members and the Part D exception and appeals process [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader because it describes the limited covered drug list and directs providers to the separate NYRx dual eligible drugs page [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says dual eligible pharmacy claims are limited by Medicare coordination rules. The policy is broadly consistent on Part D coverage, but the manual is more current and more specific about payment limits and enrollment requirements.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare and Medicaid dual eligibles, the combined payment to the pharmacy cannot exceed the amount Medicaid would have paid for a Medicaid-only patient. If Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment is made. Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For NYS Medicaid members enrolled in Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage drug plan, NYRx covers only a limited list of drugs. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan. The policy is broader and does not restate the combined payment limit.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Dual eligible Medicaid members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and prescribers must use the Part D plan exception and appeals process when a needed drug is not on the formulary.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For dual eligible members, NYRx provides coverage only for the drugs on the limited dual eligible list. All other prescription medications should be billed through Part D. This policy statement is broader and does not focus on the residential facility setting.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers must submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status. Pharmacies missing Medicare enrollments must evaluate whether CMS-855B or CMS-855S enrollment is needed before Medicaid pharmacy enrollment. The manual adds a specific Medicare enrollment requirement for pharmacy applicants.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so Medicare must be billed before Medicaid for dual enrollees. Medicaid may reimburse Medicare coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by law. The policy is broader and does not address pharmacy enrollment or assignment rules.

similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as the primary coverage source for dual eligible pharmacy claims. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three:
differences: The provider manual states the payment cap and acceptance as payment in full, while the policy source only says most nonlisted drugs must go through Part D. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three:

The provider manual also adds Medicare enrollment and assignment requirements for pharmacy providers, while the policy update does not discuss provider enrollment. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two:
answers.caveats: The policy sources are older than the current pharmacy manual, so the manual should control for current operational guidance.

https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says dual eligible members must be enrolled in Medicare Part D, Medicaid does not cover drugs covered by Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members, and total Medicare and Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount. The policy guidance is directionally consistent but narrower and older, focusing on NYRxs limited dual-eligible drug list and requiring Part D billing for other prescriptions. The provider manual is the more current source for operational guidance.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Dual eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. If Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment is made, and total Medicare and Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For NYS Medicaid members enrolled in Medicare Part D or MAPD, NYRx covers only a limited set of drugs. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan. This policy is broader in scope than the provider manual and lists examples of the dual-eligible drug coverage carve-out.

similarities: Both sources say dual eligible pharmacy coverage is limited when Medicare Part D is involved [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both sources direct most noncovered prescriptions away from Medicaid and toward the Part D drug plan [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual adds a payment cap and says Medicare and Medicaid payment together cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount, while the policy update is focused on the limited NYRx drug list and billing path [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

The provider manual is more current and states that Part D enrollment is required to maintain Medicaid coverage, while the policy update is older and does not state that requirement as directly [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

test_id_4.query_content: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For dual eligible pharmacy claims, the current provider manual says Medicare Part D coverage is primary for covered drugs, and Medicaid does not cover Part D-covered drugs for full-benefit dual eligible members. The policy evidence is consistent that NYRx only covers a limited set of dual eligible drugs, and that all other prescription medications must be billed to the Part D plan first.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For full-benefit dual eligible members, Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D. Members must use a Medicare Part D plan and its exception and appeals process for prescriptions that are not on formulary.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For NYS Medicaid members enrolled in Medicare Part D or MAPD, NYRx covers only a limited listed set of drugs. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers must submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to dual eligible members. The manual also says the combined Medicare and Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount, and Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance cannot be recovered from dually eligible individuals.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so potential third-party sources including Medicare must be billed before Medicaid. The policy guidance is broader than the manual because it states the general billing order rather than the specific pharmacy payment limit.

similarities: Both sources require Medicare to be used first for covered services before Medicaid pays [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both sources limit Medicaid payment for dual eligible pharmacy claims to a narrower secondary role [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual adds a current payment cap and a Part B cost-sharing rule, while the policy update mainly gives the dual eligible billing sequence and the limited drug coverage list [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

The policy update is broader because it describes the NYRx covered-drug carve-out, while the provider manual is more specific about full-benefit dual eligible members and the Part D exception process [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].
answers.caveats: The provider manual is the more current source overall, so it should carry more weight for current guidance.

The older policy update is consistent that Medicare Part D is the primary source for dual eligible prescription coverage, with NYRx covering only limited listed drugs and other prescriptions billed to Part D first.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For full-benefit dual eligible members, Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Medicare Part D. The member must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan and should use the plan’s exception and appeals process if a needed drug is not on the formulary.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For NYS Medicaid members enrolled in Medicare Part D or Medicare Advantage Drug Contracting plans, NYRx covers only a limited list of drugs. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan.

similarities: Both sources make Medicare Part D the primary payer for dual eligible pharmacy claims for covered drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both sources say that drugs outside the Medicaid or NYRx exception list should be billed to the Part D plan first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

test_id_6.query_content: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• If date of notification of eligibility is after 90 days of DOS, LTC/FC facility pharmacies may send the claim
information for retro-PA consideration, within 30 days from the date of notification of eligibility, to the
Medicaid Pharmacy unit at PPNO@health.ny.gov. All claims submitted for PA after 30 days of
eligibility determination will be denied. Additional information on submitting claims over 90 from DOS
June 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/QuickRefDocs/FOD-7001_Sub_Claims_Over_90_
days_Old.pdf.
• Any claim submitted with a DOS after the date of notification of eligibility will be denied. The claim should
have been billed on the DOS.
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility as well as the pharmacy, to actively and
regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their members.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which may include the facility,
commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Additional information regarding timely billing can be found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so other available coverage must be billed first. In the current provider manual, dual-eligible members must be enrolled in Medicare Part D, and Medicaid does not cover Part D drugs for full-benefit dual eligibles.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For dual-eligible members, Medicare Part D enrollment is required to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligibles. The combined Medicare and Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount, and the payment must be accepted as payment in full.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid requires providers to bill applicable third parties before Medicaid, because Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer and submit claims under the appropriate coverage benefit. The policy is broader than the manual because it states the general coordination rule rather than the dual-eligible Part D rule.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If a member has an open Workers’ Compensation case, that coverage must be used before billing NYRx Medicaid. If the drug claim is unrelated to the work injury, NYRx may be billed as primary payer for that unrelated claim. The manual gives a specific exception for unrelated maintenance medications.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must coordinate with the proper primary payer and use the appropriate coverage benefit before billing NYS Medicaid. The policy does not give the Workers’ Compensation example. It is less specific than the manual on when Medicaid may become primary.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The provider must determine whether the patient has other insurance and whether that insurance covers the service being billed. If the service is covered, or if coverage is uncertain, the provider must submit the claim to the other insurer before billing Medicaid. Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must exhaust all existing benefits and bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage first. The policy states the same billing-first principle, but it is less detailed about how to handle uncertain coverage.

similarities: Both sources say Medicaid pays after other responsible coverage is used first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
Both sources require coordination with the proper primary payer before submitting the Medicaid claim [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is more specific for dual-eligible pharmacy claims because it ties the rule to Medicare Part D enrollment and excludes Part D-covered drugs for full-benefit dual eligibles [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader because it states the general coordination of benefits rule and does not focus on Medicare Part D or dual-eligible drug coverage [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• If date of notification of eligibility is after 90 days of DOS, LTC/FC facility pharmacies may send the claim
information for retro-PA consideration, within 30 days from the date of notification of eligibility, to the
Medicaid Pharmacy unit at PPNO@health.ny.gov. All claims submitted for PA after 30 days of
eligibility determination will be denied. Additional information on submitting claims over 90 from DOS
June 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/QuickRefDocs/FOD-7001_Sub_Claims_Over_90_
days_Old.pdf.
• Any claim submitted with a DOS after the date of notification of eligibility will be denied. The claim should
have been billed on the DOS.
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility as well as the pharmacy, to actively and
regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their members.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which may include the facility,
commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Additional information regarding timely billing can be found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicaid is generally the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must bill Medicare or other liable third parties first, and Medicaid only pays after those resources are used.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare and Medicaid dual eligibles, the manual says Medicare payment and any Medicaid payment are accepted as payment in full. It also says Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. This makes Medicare the primary payer pathway for those covered Part D drugs.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid requires providers to bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid, because Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer first. The policy is broader than the manual because it addresses all liable third parties, not only Medicare Part.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If other insurance exists, the provider must determine whether that coverage applies before billing Medicaid. The billing guidance says the other insurer must be billed first when the service is covered or coverage is unknown. This reflects a primary payer-first rule for pharmacy claims.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must exhaust all existing benefits before billing NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. The policy is broader because it applies to all available benefits, not only pharmacy claims for dual eligibles.

similarities: Both sources say other available coverage must be billed before NYS Medicaid pays [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary or last resort when another payer is available [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about dual eligibles and Medicare Part D, while the policy update states a broader coordination of benefits rule for all liable third parties [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The manual also says full-benefit dual eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan, which the policy update does not address [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicaid is the payer of last resort and other applicable coverage must be billed first. The provider manual also says full-benefit dual eligibles must be enrolled in Medicare Part D, and Medicaid does not cover Part D-covered drug classes.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The pharmacy manual says Medicare and Medicaid dual eligibles must be enrolled in Medicare Part D to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any drug class covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and prescribers must use a Part D-covered drug or the Part D exception and appeals process first.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid says providers must bill applicable liable third parties before billing Medicaid. Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer first.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The manual gives a narrower coordination rule for other responsible coverage. If another payer is responsible, that coverage must be used before NYRx Medicaid, even though the cited chunk is specific to Medicare Part D rather than all third parties. The manual frames this as a current coverage limitation for dual eligibles.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update states the same general rule more broadly. Providers must exhaust all existing benefits before billing NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The billing guidance also supports the same payer-order rule. It says providers must determine whether the patient has other insurance and whether that insurance covers the service being billed. If the service is covered, or coverage is unknown, the provider must bill the other insurer before Medicaid.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update is broader and less operational than the manual. It says pharmacies must submit claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. It does not give the manuals billing-test detail for whether the service is covered.

similarities: Both sources say Medicaid pays last and another responsible payer must be used first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Both sources require coordination with the proper primary payer before Medicaid is billed [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines].
differences: The provider manual is more specific for dual eligibles because it ties the rule to Medicare Part D enrollment and Part D-covered drug classes, while the policy update states the payer-of-last-resort rule more generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
answers.caveats: The approved row plan includes one strongly matching dual-eligible row and two broader coordination rows that support the same payer-order principle.

using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP

• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which may include the facility,
commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Additional information regarding timely billing can be found at:

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so other liable coverage must be billed first. The provider manual also says full-benefit dual eligible members must be enrolled in Medicare Part D, and Medicaid does not cover Part D drug classes.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare and Medicaid dual eligibles, Medicaid does not pay before Medicare or Part D when those benefits apply. The manual says the total Medicare and Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount. It also says full-benefit dual eligible members must be enrolled in Medicare Part D, and Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Part.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: New York State Medicaid says providers must bill liable third parties before billing Medicaid. Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer before submitting the claim, but the policy does not add the Part D enrollment rule.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The manual gives a specific exception example for workers’ compensation claims. If the drug claim is not related to the open workers’ compensation case, NYRx may be billed as primary payer using Eligibility Clarification Code 2 in field 309-C9. The manual still says Medicaid is the payer of last resort when another responsible payer applies.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The update states the broader coordination of benefits rule. Providers must exhaust existing benefits before billing Medicaid. It does not give the workers’ compensation example or the field-level billing instruction.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The billing guidance says the provider must determine whether the member has other insurance and whether that insurance covers the service. If the service is covered, or if coverage is unknown, the provider must bill the other insurer before Medicaid. Medicaid remains the payer of last resort.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The update says pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to Medicaid. It also says providers must ask about other third-party coverage and coordinate with the proper primary payer. The policy is broader and does not restate the manual’s billing-screening detail.

similarities: Both sources say other liable coverage must be billed before Medicaid, because Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines].

Both sources place the coordination of benefits responsibility on the pharmacy or provider before Medicaid is billed [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines].
differences: The provider manual is more specific because it adds a Medicare Part D rule for full-benefit dual eligibles and says Medicaid does not cover Part D drug classes [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader because it states the last-resort rule and primary payer coordination without the manual’s Part D enrollment and coverage limits [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual also includes a workers’ compensation exception example and a specific field reference, while the policy update does not [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –

lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and

(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so the pharmacy must use Medicare or other applicable coverage first. The provider manual also says full-benefit dual eligibles must be enrolled in Medicare Part D, and Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Part.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For full-benefit dual eligibles, Medicare Part D enrollment is required to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members, and members should use the Part D plan or its exception and appeals process first.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid, and pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer. The policy is broader than the manual because it does not name Part D specifically, but it does require use of the appropriate medical or pharmacy coverage benefit first.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: When a member has an open workers' compensation case for a work-related injury, workers' compensation coverage must be used before billing NYRx Medicaid. If the drug is unrelated to that case, NYRx may be billed as primary, and Medicaid remains the payer of last resort otherwise.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must bill applicable third parties that may be liable before billing Medicaid. This policy statement is broader than the manual because it does not focus on workers' compensation, but it reflects the same last-resort coordination-of-benefits rule.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The pharmacy must check whether other insurance exists and whether that insurance covers the service being billed. If the service is covered or coverage is unknown, the claim must go to the other insurer before Medicaid because Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid says providers and pharmacies must bill all applicable third parties before billing Medicaid. The policy confirms that Medicaid pays only after other available resources are used.

similarities: Both sources require other available coverage to be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines].

Both sources treat Medicaid as the payer of last resort for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines].
differences: The provider manual is more specific for dual eligibles because it says Part D enrollment is required and Part D drugs are not covered for full-benefit dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader because it speaks to coordination of benefits and the proper primary payer, but it does not name Medicare Part D [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
answers.caveats: The policy source is broader than the provider manual on dual-eligible pharmacy claims, so the comparison reflects a general primary-payer rule rather than a separate pharmacy-specific dual-eligible rule.

test_id_11.query_content: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual says that for Medicare and Medicaid dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must treat the combined payment as payment in full and cannot seek Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from the member. The Medicaid policy is narrower and more general, because it says providers cannot directly recover Medicare cost-sharing from dual-eligible individuals and that any waiver of copayment collection must be case by case based on financial need.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare and Medicaid dual-eligible individuals, the pharmacy must accept Medicare and Medicaid payment as payment in full. The pharmacy cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from the member. This is stated as a pharmacy billing rule for the Part B benefit.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: A provider cannot attempt to recover Medicare Part C copayment or coinsurance directly from dual-eligible individuals. The provider must accept Medicare plan payment and any New York State Medicaid payment as payment in full, and the member may not be billed for any unreimbursed amount. This policy example is narrower because it addresses Medicare Part C rather than the broader pharmacy Part B rule.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacy providers may not refuse service to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford the copayment. The manual ties this to co-payments for drugs and medical supplies. It states the service obligation clearly, but it does not describe a general proactive waiver rule.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Waivers are allowed only case by case based on financial need at the members request. The policy also says providers may not refuse service to eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford the copayment.

similarities: Both sources say the member should not be left responsible for the unpaid Medicare cost-sharing amount when the dual-eligible billing rule applies [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both sources also require continued access to service when a Medicaid member cannot afford the copayment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
differences: The provider manual gives a pharmacy-specific Part B rule and says the accepted Medicare and Medicaid payment is payment in full, while the policy update gives a broader cost-sharing rule and illustrates it with Medicare Part C [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

The policy update separately addresses when copays may be waived, and it limits waivers to case-by-case financial need, while the provider manual mainly says the pharmacy may not refuse service to members who cannot pay [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
