• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual says Medicare and Medicaid dual-eligible members cannot be billed for Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts, and payment must be accepted as payment in full. The policy evidence is only partially aligned because it gives the same no-balance-billing rule for Medicare Advantage cost-sharing, but it does not directly address the same Part B dual-eligible pharmacy rule.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare Part B benefits, a pharmacy provider cannot recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For Medicare Advantage dual-eligible claims, the provider must accept the Medicare Part C plan payment and any New York State Medicaid payment as payment in full. The member may not be billed for any co-payment or co-insurance amount that is not reimbursed by Medicaid.

similarities: Both sources prohibit billing the dual-eligible member for unpaid cost-sharing when the payer amount is not fully reimbursed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
differences: The provider manual is specific to Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance in the pharmacy setting, while the policy update is broader and addresses Medicare Advantage co-payment and co-insurance for Part C claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
answers.caveats: The policy update is only a partial match because it covers Medicare Advantage cost-sharing rather than the same Medicare Part B pharmacy rule.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says dual-eligible members generally must accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full, and pharmacies may not seek Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from those individuals. The retrieved Medicaid policy excerpt is broader and only says duals are subject to Medicare rules, so it does not give the same specific cost-sharing instruction.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare and Medicaid dual-eligible individuals, the pharmacy must accept Medicare and Medicaid payment as payment in full. The pharmacy may not recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from the member. The manual also says total payment cannot exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The retrieved Medicaid Update excerpt says duals are subject to Medicare rules. It does not give the same direct instruction about collecting or not collecting Medicare cost-sharing from the member. The policy statement is therefore broader and less specific on this point.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid pharmacy providers must serve otherwise eligible members even if they cannot afford the copayment. The manual says providers may not refuse services to a Medicaid member who cannot pay the copayment. This rule applies to drugs and medical supplies in the cited section.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The retrieved policy excerpt does not discuss copayment refusal or inability to pay. It only refers generally to duals being subject to Medicare rules. Because of that, it does not confirm or negate the manual’s separate Medicaid copayment rule.

similarities: Both sources touch on dual-eligible members and member cost-sharing rules. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
differences: The provider manual gives a specific prohibition on recovering Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance from dual-eligible individuals, while the policy excerpt only gives a general statement that duals are subject to Medicare rules. [New York State Medicaid Program:
answers.caveats: The retrieved policy excerpt is partial for this topic, so it does not directly address dual-eligible Medicare cost-sharing or the Medicaid copayment refusal rule in the same level of detail as the provider manual.

• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual is clearer and newer. For Medicare Part B services, a pharmacy cannot seek deductible or coinsurance from Medicare and Medicaid dual-eligible individuals, and the combined Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full. The policy is only partially aligned because it addresses a Medicare Advantage Part C billing scenario, but it says the same general thing about cost-sharing and payment in full.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare Part B benefits, a pharmacy provider cannot recover deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare and Medicaid dual-eligible individuals. The Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. The manual also states that total payment to the pharmacy cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For Medicare Advantage Part C claims, the provider may not try to recover copayment or coinsurance directly from Medicare and New York State Medicaid dual-eligible individuals. The provider must accept the Medicare plan payment and any Medicaid payment as payment in full. The policy is narrower because it uses a Part C example rather than the Part B rule in the manual.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The pharmacy program charges copayments for many drug and medical supply items. Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to pay the copayment. This rule is about access to service and is not limited to dual-eligible members.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy chunk does not address pharmacy refusal of service for members who cannot afford copayments. It instead says providers cannot proactively waive copays, and waivers are allowed only case by case based on financial need at the members request. That makes it a different and less direct rule than the manual guidance.

similarities: Both sources say dual-eligible members cannot be billed for unreimbursed Medicare-related cost-sharing, and payment in full must come from the Medicare and Medicaid payments together [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both sources focus on the providers billing responsibility rather than leaving the cost-sharing balance with the member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
differences: The provider manual gives the broader current rule for Medicare Part B benefits, while the policy update uses a narrower Medicare Advantage Part C example [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

The provider manual also separately says pharmacies may not refuse service to Medicaid members who cannot afford copayments, but the policy update instead limits when copays may be waived [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
answers.caveats: The policy evidence is only a partial match because it addresses Medicare Part C billing, while the newer provider manual states the current Part B rule.

policy.text:
Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing. These items
may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid.

Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document, found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/DME_Procedure_Codes.pdf, are not subject

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Information
regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2020 Volume 36 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says Medicaid generally does not pay for prescription drugs for Medicare Part D members. It only pays for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs List that are specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid should not be billed for most prescription drugs for a Medicare Part D member. Medicaid only pays for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs List when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage. Examples include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid may pay the Medicaid allowed amount for drugs in classes specifically excluded from coverage under the third-party liability plan, including Medicare. The policy statement is broader and does not limit the point to the Part D list language used in the manual. It is the closest match for when excluded drugs may be paid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For full-benefit dual eligible members, Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D. Members and prescribers must use the Part D plan exception and appeals process for needed drugs that are not on the formulary. The manual also says claims submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: A zero-fill response from a third-party liability plan does not end the billing process. The claim must still be submitted, and the pharmacy must ensure it was adjudicated under the correct coverage benefit process, such as pharmacy or medical billing with National Drug Code or Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System as appropriate. This is more operational than the manual guidance.

similarities: Both sources treat Medicare Part D coverage as the starting point and allow Medicaid only for excluded drug classes or exceptions, not for routine Part D-covered drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both sources place the Medicaid rule inside a broader coordination-of-benefits framework for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about current Part D member limits and names examples of covered exclusions, while the policy update is broader and focuses on excluded drug classes under third-party liability [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

The policy update adds claim-processing instructions for zero-fill responses and correct submission pathways, while the provider manual emphasizes plan exceptions, appeals, and prior authorization instead [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
answers.caveats: The policy update is older than the provider manual, so the manual should be treated as the current controlling guidance for this comparison.

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says Medicaid generally should not be billed for Medicare Part D members’ prescription drugs, except for drugs Medicaid can cover when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D. The older Medicaid Update reaches a similar result, but it frames payment as coordination of benefits for drugs that are excluded from Medicare or other third-party liability coverage.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless the drug is on the Medicaid reimbursable list and its drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D. The manual gives examples such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. All dual-eligible claims remain subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Through coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid-coverable claims, or it will pay up to Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from the third-party liability plan, including Medicare. The policy is broader than the manual because it speaks to TPL and Medicare generally, rather than only the Part D billing exception. It also requires proper TPL adjudication and submission under the appropriate benefit path.

similarities: Both sources allow Medicaid payment when Medicare or Part D does not cover the drug class. Both also tie payment to proper Medicaid processing rules. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines;
differences: The provider manual is narrower and says Medicaid should not be billed for Part D members’ drugs except for specific Part D-law exclusions. The Medicaid Update is broader and describes coordination-of-benefits payment for drugs excluded from the third-party liability plan, including Medicare. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines;
answers.caveats: The provider manual is the more current source and should control current billing guidance. The policy update is still useful because it shows the older coordination-of-benefits framing.

no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says Medicaid generally does not pay for Medicare Part D drugs for dual-eligible members, except for drugs the law specifically excludes from Part D coverage, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. The 2021 Medicaid Update is consistent on the core rule but adds operational detail about how excluded drug claims should be processed.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not pay for prescription drugs for Medicare Part D members unless the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage. The manual gives examples such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. It also says claims for dual-eligible members remain subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or pay up to the Medicaid allowed amount for drugs in classes specifically excluded from coverage under the third-party liability plan, including Medicare. This is broader than the manual because it describes the payment method, not just the coverage rule. It also applies the rule through coordination of benefits.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For dual-eligible members, Medicaid is not the payer for classes of drugs covered under Medicare Part D. Members and prescribers should use the Part D exception and appeals process for covered drugs, and Medicaid remains available only for excluded classes. The manual does not lay out the zero-fill claim submission steps in this section.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: If a claim gets a zero-fill reimbursement from a third-party liability plan, it still must be submitted to NYS Medicaid. The provider must also make sure the claim was adjudicated under the correct third-party liability claim process, including the correct benefit and code set. This is more operational than the manuals coverage-only guidance.

similarities: Both sources say Medicaid does not pay for Medicare Part D covered drugs for dual-eligible members, and both recognize an exception for drugs excluded from Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both sources tie the exception to excluded drug classes rather than to ordinary Part D covered drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
differences: The provider manual focuses on the current coverage rule and examples of excluded drugs, while the Medicaid Update adds claim payment and submission instructions for third-party liability and zero-fill responses [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

The policy update is broader because it explains that excluded drug claims may be paid up to the Medicaid allowed amount and must still be submitted through the correct claim process, including the proper benefit and code family [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.

Both sources tie coverage to drugs or drug classes that are specifically excluded from the other plans coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

test_id_21.query_content: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_21.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Under the current Pharmacy Manual, a pharmacy enrolled as a Medicaid billing provider must bill Medicare first for services covered by both programs, and it may bill Medicaid only after Medicare payment information is received. The policy update says the same basic rule in broader coordination of benefits terms: pharmacies must bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid because Medicaid is the payer of last resort.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers must maximize Medicare benefits first. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare, and Medicaid can be billed only after Medicare payment information is received. The manual also says the pharmacy must bill the appropriate Medicare benefit and may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies must submit claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid because Medicaid is the payer of last resort. This policy statement is broader and does not single out Medicare.

similarities: Both sources require the pharmacy to pursue other available coverage before billing NYS Medicaid or Medicaid, which includes Medicare when it applies [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary or last in line after other payment sources are used [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is more specific because it names Medicare and says Medicaid may be billed only after Medicare payment information is received, while the policy update states the broader third-party liability rule and does not spell out Medicare sequencing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
answers.caveats: The policy update is broader than the provider manual and is not limited to Medicare-covered pharmacy claims.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual rules say pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers must bill Medicare first when a service is covered by both programs. Medicaid may be billed only after Medicare payment information is received, and the policy states the broader payer-of-last-resort rule.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicaid-enrolled pharmacies, Medicare benefits must be maximized before Medicaid is billed. If a service is covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare. Medicaid billing is allowed only after Medicare payment information is received.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid because Medicaid is the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must submit claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. The policy is broader because it applies to all liable third parties, not just Medicare.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If the service is covered, or if coverage is unknown, the provider must bill the other insurance carrier before billing Medicaid. The billing rule is tied to the payer-of-last-resort standard. The provider manual also says the claim must follow the appropriate Medicare or other primary billing pathway.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must exhaust all existing benefits before billing NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies should ask whether the member has other third-party coverage to ensure proper coordination of benefits. The policy gives the general coordination step, but it is less specific than the pharmacy manual.

similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be billed before Medicaid when primary coverage exists [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
Both sources treat Medicaid as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is more specific because it says Medicare must be billed first for services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, while the policy states a broader third-party rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual adds that Medicaid billing can happen only after Medicare payment information is received, while the policy does not give that Medicare-specific timing detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

similarities: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before NYS Medicaid is billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources treat Medicaid as the payer of last resort for these pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The Pharmacy Manual is more specific because it says Medicaid pharmacy billing providers must show Medicare participation and must bill Medicare first for services covered by both programs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader because it describes the general coordination-of-benefits rule and does not single out Medicare in the same operational detail [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of

compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
