provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For crossover claims, Medicaid prior approval is not required after Medicare has approved and paid the service or procedure. This applies even when Medicaid would otherwise have required prior approval.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicaid and Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for the service or procedure. This rule applies even when the service would otherwise have required Medicaid prior approval. The manual states this as current guidance for crossover claims.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must resolve claim issues with the third party before submitting to Medicaid, including prior authorization requirements. The policy also says Medicare-covered claims are billed to Medicare first. It is broader than the manual and does not state the crossover prior approval exception as directly.

similarities: Both sources require Medicare to be billed first when Medicare is involved.
Both sources support the general rule that third-party requirements must be addressed before Medicaid payment is considered [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual gives a specific crossover exception and says prior approval is not required after Medicare has approved and paid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
The policy update is broader and only says prior authorization issues must be handled with the third party before Medicaid submission [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare

When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For crossover claims, prior approval is not required after Medicaid payment if Medicare approved and paid for the service or procedure. This applies even when Medicaid prior approval would otherwise have been required.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicaid and Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service or procedure. This applies even when the service would otherwise have needed Medicaid prior approval. The guidance is specific to crossover claims and ties the exception to Medicare payment.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must first bill Medicare or other third-party coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid. The policy source is broader and says claim issues, including prior authorization requirements, must be resolved with the third party first. It does not state the crossover exception as directly as the provider manual.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The provider manual treats crossover claims as a special case within the prior approval rule. If Medicare has already approved and paid, Medicaid prior approval is not required for that claim. The rule is narrow and focused on the Medicare-paid crossover pathway.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update explains the general third-party-first rule for Medicaid billing. It says members with Medicare or other insurance must be billed first, and Medicaid is the payor of last resort. It is less specific about prior approval after Medicare payment on crossover claims.

similarities: Both sources recognize that Medicare or other third-party coverage must be addressed before Medicaid claims are submitted [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15].

Both sources treat prior authorization or prior approval as part of the third-party billing workflow when another payer is involved [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual gives a direct crossover exception and says prior approval is not required after Medicare approval and payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines]. The policy update states the broader rule that Medicare or other third-party coverage must be billed first and that Medicaid claim issues should be resolved with the third party before submission [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
answers.caveats: The policy source is broader and less specific than the provider manual on this exact crossover-claim exception.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.

It is broader and less specific than the manual because it does not address prior approval directly. It still shows that crossover claims are treated differently from ordinary third-party claims.

similarities: Both sources treat Medicare crossover claims as a special exception from ordinary Medicaid claim rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15].
differences: The provider manual answers the prior-approval question directly, while the policy update addresses only the EOB attachment rule and is therefore less specific [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15].

test_id_31.query_content: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Additional information regarding Coordination
of Benefits (COB) can be found in the following Medicaid Update articles:
• Update on Coordination of Benefits Submissions for Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing article
published in the October 2022 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://
www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/docs/mu_no12_oct22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits article published in the April 2022 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/
docs/mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients with Other Coverage article published in the
November 2021 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/
health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/docs/mu_no13_nov21_pr.pdf; and
• Attention Pharmacy Providers: Coordination of Benefits (COB) Processing Change for Other Payer
ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier article published in the October 2017 issue of the Medicaid Update,
located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/oct17_mu.pdf.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
o Medicaid members younger than 21 years old.
o Medicaid members during pregnancy and for the two months after the month in which the
pregnancy ends.
o Residents of Adult Care Facilities licensed by the New York State Department of Health (DOH).
o Residents of nursing homes.
o Residents of Intermediate Care Facilities for the Developmentally Disabled (ICF/DD).
o Residents of Office of Mental Health (OMH) or Office of Persons with Developmental Disabilities
(OPWDD) certified residences.
o Members in Comprehensive Medicaid Case Management (CMCM) or Service Coordination
Programs.
o Members in OMH or OPWDD Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) Waiver Programs. and
o Members in a DOH HCBS Waiver Program for Persons with Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI).
o Members with incomes below 100 percent of the federal poverty level.
o Members in Hospice.
o American Indians and Alaska Natives who have ever received a service from the Indian Health
Service, tribal health programs or under contract health services referral.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For Coordination of Benefits, NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort. Providers and pharmacies must bill any liable third party first and use all available coverage before billing Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If a member has an open workers' compensation case for a work-related injury, workers' compensation coverage must be used before NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program billing. If the claim is not related to the workers' compensation case, NYRx may be billed as primary with Eligibility Clarification Code 2 in field 309-C9. Medicaid is still the payer of last resort, and all other responsible payers must be included on the claim if applicable.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid and use the proper primary payer. The policy is broader than the manual because it does not spell out the workers' compensation exception. It also says pharmacies must submit under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: All available resources must be used before Medicaid pays. A responsible third party must pay first, and Medicaid is the payer of last resort. Claims with other coverage may deny at point of service, and correctly submitted claims can receive patient responsibility or up to the Medicaid allowed amount for certain excluded drugs.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid and exhaust existing benefits first. NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted coverable claims, up to the Medicaid allowed amount. The policy also instructs providers to ask about other third-party coverage and to coordinate with the proper primary payer.

similarities: Both sources say other liable coverage must be billed before Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4]
differences: The provider manual adds a specific workers' compensation rule and an Eligibility Clarification Code 2 detail, while the policy update states only the broader third-party billing requirement. [New York State Medicaid Program:

answers.headline_summary: Coordination of Benefits requires Medicaid to be billed only after all other responsible third parties have been used, because NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort. For pharmacy claims, providers must bill the primary payer first and submit Medicaid only for correctly processed patient responsibility or other allowed Medicaid-covered amounts.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For a work-related injury with an open workers’ compensation case, workers’ compensation must be billed before NYRx Medicaid. If the drug claim is not related to that case, NYRx may be billed as primary with Eligibility Clarification Code 2 in field 309-C9. All other responsible payers still must be included on the claim, if applicable.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update is broader, but it still requires applicable third parties to be billed before NYS Medicaid. It does not specifically discuss workers’ compensation. It also says pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer and use the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Federal rules require all available resources to be used before Medicaid pays. Providers must pursue any available other coverage first, and any responsible third party must pay first. Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update says providers must bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid. It also says all existing benefits must be exhausted first, and that providers should ask members about other third-party coverage. This matches the provider manual, but it is stated more generally.

similarities: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
Both sources treat NYS Medicaid as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual gives a specific workers’ compensation exception and field code detail, while the policy update stays broader and does not mention that exception [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The policy update explicitly says pharmacies should ask members about other third-party coverage, while the provider manual focuses more on the billing sequence and the claim outcome [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

test_id_34.query_content: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Additional information regarding Coordination
of Benefits (COB) can be found in the following Medicaid Update articles:
• Update on Coordination of Benefits Submissions for Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing article
published in the October 2022 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://
www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/docs/mu_no12_oct22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits article published in the April 2022 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/
docs/mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients with Other Coverage article published in the
November 2021 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/
health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/docs/mu_no13_nov21_pr.pdf; and
• Attention Pharmacy Providers: Coordination of Benefits (COB) Processing Change for Other Payer
ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier article published in the October 2017 issue of the Medicaid Update,
located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/oct17_mu.pdf.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
o Medicaid members younger than 21 years old.
o Medicaid members during pregnancy and for the two months after the month in which the
pregnancy ends.
o Residents of Adult Care Facilities licensed by the New York State Department of Health (DOH).
o Residents of nursing homes.
o Residents of Intermediate Care Facilities for the Developmentally Disabled (ICF/DD).
o Residents of Office of Mental Health (OMH) or Office of Persons with Developmental Disabilities
(OPWDD) certified residences.
o Members in Comprehensive Medicaid Case Management (CMCM) or Service Coordination
Programs.
o Members in OMH or OPWDD Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) Waiver Programs. and
o Members in a DOH HCBS Waiver Program for Persons with Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI).
o Members with incomes below 100 percent of the federal poverty level.
o Members in Hospice.
o American Indians and Alaska Natives who have ever received a service from the Indian Health
Service, tribal health programs or under contract health services referral.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so providers must bill any responsible third party first and coordinate benefits before billing Medicaid. If another payer is responsible, Medicaid then pays only the member’s patient responsibility or the Medicaid-allowed amount, subject to correct claim submission.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Federal and state rules require providers to pursue any available other coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and a responsible third party must pay first.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid, and pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage first. The policy statement is broader because it explains the general pharmacy workflow, not just the legal rule.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For a work-related injury with an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be used before billing NYRx Medicaid. If the drug is not related to that case, NYRx may be billed as primary with Eligibility Clarification Code 2, and other responsible payers must still be included if applicable.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy says providers must bill other liable coverage first and coordinate with the proper primary payer. It is less specific because it does not give the Workers’ Compensation exception or the Eligibility Clarification Code 2 instruction.

similarities: Both sources say Medicaid is the payer of last resort and that other available coverage must be used first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources say claims must be coordinated with the primary payer before Medicaid is billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual adds a Workers’ Compensation exception and a coding instruction for unrelated drugs, while the policy update stays at a general coordination of benefits level [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual also states that Medicaid may pay patient responsibility or up to the Medicaid allowed amount for certain claims, while the policy update frames the same rule more generally for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

answers.headline_summary: For NYRx Medicaid, Coordination of Benefits requires billing any liable third party first because Medicaid is the payer of last resort. A special Workers’ Compensation rule applies when the claim is related to an open work-related injury case.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If a member has an open Workers’ Compensation case for a work-related injury, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be used before billing NYRx Medicaid. If the drug is not related to that case, NYRx may be billed as primary with Eligibility Clarification Code 2 in field 309-C9. Medicaid remains the payer of last resort, and other responsible payers must be included on the claim when applicable.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy says providers must bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid and exhaust existing benefits first. It is broader than the provider manual and does not give the Workers’ Compensation exception. It also says pharmacies should coordinate with the proper primary payer [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Federal law and New York Medicaid rules require providers to pursue any available other coverage before submitting to Medicaid. If a responsible third party is primary, that payer must pay first. Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted claim or pay up to the Medicaid allowed amount for certain excluded drug classes, including Medicare-related exclusions.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy requires pharmacies to bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid and to ask members about other coverage. It also says NYS Medicaid will pay patient responsibility on correctly submitted coverable claims, up to the allowed amount. This matches the core rule and is slightly less specific about the federal and regulatory basis [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

similarities: Both sources say other liable coverage must be billed before Medicaid, and Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
Both sources say the primary payer must be coordinated with before NYS Medicaid pays [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual adds a specific Workers’ Compensation exception and the Eligibility Clarification Code 2 billing instruction, while the policy update stays broader and does not address that exception [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

test_id_36.query_content: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_36.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacists may work with the patient and contact their prescriber to either change the
prescription to another drug or supply or obtain a prior authorization (PA) following the transition fill.
New York State Medicaid Update Special Edition – Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition pg. 4

Transition fills will be identified in the eMedNY claim response using NCPDP field 548-6F, Approved
Message Code, with code “005” — Claim paid under the plan's transition benefit period, otherwise claim
would have rejected as PA is required.
Vaccines Administered by Pharmacists
As indicated in the Scope of Benefits document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/
redesign/mrt2/pharmacy_transition/repository/docs/rx_scope_of_benefits.pdf, vaccines that are administered
by pharmacists are subject to the benefit transition and should be billed to NYRx. Policy and billing guidance
for vaccines can be found within the New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacists as
Immunizers Fact Sheet, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/docs/phar
_immun_fact.pdf.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Required Prescribing Information
In accordance with NY State Education Law, all prescriptions written in New York State by a person authorized by New
York State to issue such prescriptions shall be transmitted electronically directly from prescriber to pharmacist in a
licensed pharmacy. Official New York State prescription forms or an oral prescription are accepted when exceptions
exist as noted in law.
All prescriptions and fiscal orders must bear:
• The name, address, age, and client identification number (CIN) of the patient for whom it is intended. If the
CIN does not appear on the order, the prescription should only be filled if the CIN is readily available in the
pharmacy records.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: When required patient information is missing, the pharmacist does not always have to write it on the prescription if the information is already available in pharmacy records or the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate. For prescriber-related issues, the pharmacist must make a good-faith effort to verify the prescription’s validity and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown, and should consult the prescriber as needed about prescription directions.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If the Medicaid member’s address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter it if the information is readily available in pharmacy records. The pharmacist may also leave it off if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes. This policy is broader and does not specifically address missing patient identifiers.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The pharmacist must make a good-faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown. This is a pharmacist responsibility tied to prescriber uncertainty.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes. This is related, but it is less specific than the manual’s rule about an unknown prescriber.

similarities: Both sources require the pharmacist or provider to check the prescription before acting on it [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

Both sources allow consultation with the prescriber when information needs to be confirmed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual gives a specific rule for missing member address, age, or CIN, while the policy only speaks generally about verifying directions for use [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

The provider manual also adds a good-faith duty to verify an unknown prescriber’s identity, but the policy does not mention unknown prescribers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
answers.caveats: The policy source is broader than the provider manual on this topic, so the manual is the better source for the specific responsibilities when patient or prescriber information is missing.

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacists may work with the patient and contact their prescriber to either change the
prescription to another drug or supply or obtain a prior authorization (PA) following the transition fill.
New York State Medicaid Update Special Edition – Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition pg. 4

Transition fills will be identified in the eMedNY claim response using NCPDP field 548-6F, Approved
Message Code, with code “005” — Claim paid under the plan's transition benefit period, otherwise claim
would have rejected as PA is required.
Vaccines Administered by Pharmacists
As indicated in the Scope of Benefits document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/
redesign/mrt2/pharmacy_transition/repository/docs/rx_scope_of_benefits.pdf, vaccines that are administered
by pharmacists are subject to the benefit transition and should be billed to NYRx. Policy and billing guidance
for vaccines can be found within the New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacists as
Immunizers Fact Sheet, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/docs/phar
_immun_fact.pdf.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Required Prescribing Information
In accordance with NY State Education Law, all prescriptions written in New York State by a person authorized by New
York State to issue such prescriptions shall be transmitted electronically directly from prescriber to pharmacist in a
licensed pharmacy. Official New York State prescription forms or an oral prescription are accepted when exceptions
exist as noted in law.
All prescriptions and fiscal orders must bear:
• The name, address, age, and client identification number (CIN) of the patient for whom it is intended. If the
CIN does not appear on the order, the prescription should only be filled if the CIN is readily available in the
pharmacy records.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: When required patient information is missing, the pharmacist may still fill the prescription if the missing address, age, or CIN is already available in pharmacy records or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. For prescriber-related gaps, the pharmacist must make a good faith effort to verify the prescription and the prescriber’s identity, and may need to consult the prescriber about changes.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If the Medicaid member’s address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter those items on the prescription if they are already readily available in pharmacy records. The pharmacist may also proceed if otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update is broader and does not address missing patient identifiers directly. It says providers should verify the directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist, the pharmacist must make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity. This is a specific verification duty tied to unknown prescribers.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update is less specific on prescriber identity. It only says providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes.

similarities: Both sources require pharmacist follow-up when a prescription is incomplete or needs clarification [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

Both sources allow action that depends on checking the prescription with available information or contacting the prescriber when needed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
differences: The manual gives a specific rule for missing patient address, age, or CIN, while the policy update only gives a general direction to verify use directions and consult the prescriber [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

The manual expressly requires a good faith effort to verify the prescription and prescriber identity when the prescriber is unknown, while the policy update does not state that standard [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

For prescriber-related gaps, the pharmacist must make a good faith effort to verify the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown, and should consult the prescriber as necessary on prescription changes.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If the Medicaid member’s address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter it if the information is already readily available in the pharmacy records. The pharmacist may also rely on being otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate. This rule is specific to member-identifying information.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on any changes. aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist, the pharmacist must make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity. aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on any changes. The provider manual requires a good faith effort to verify the prescriber’s identity when the prescriber is unknown, but the policy does not state that identity-verification rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
