provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Required Prescribing Information
In accordance with NY State Education Law, all prescriptions written in New York State by a person authorized by New
York State to issue such prescriptions shall be transmitted electronically directly from prescriber to pharmacist in a
licensed pharmacy. Official New York State prescription forms or an oral prescription are accepted when exceptions
exist as noted in law.
All prescriptions and fiscal orders must bear:
• The name, address, age, and client identification number (CIN) of the patient for whom it is intended. If the
CIN does not appear on the order, the prescription should only be filled if the CIN is readily available in the
pharmacy records.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: When required patient information is missing, pharmacists do not have to enter the missing address, age, or CIN if that information is already readily available in pharmacy records or if the prescription is otherwise legitimate. For prescriber-related issues, pharmacists must make a good faith effort to verify the prescription’s validity and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown, and they should consult the prescriber when directions need clarification or changes.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If the Medicaid member’s address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to add it to the prescription when the information is readily available in the pharmacy records. The pharmacist also does not have to add it if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate. This rule is limited to those listed patient identifiers and does not override other prescription requirements.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update is broader and less specific on missing patient identifiers. It says providers should verify prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes. It does not address address, age, or CIN entry rules.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist, the pharmacist must make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity. This is an active verification responsibility, not a passive record check. The manual ties the duty to situations where the prescriber is unknown.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update is a partial match because it focuses on directions for use and consultation about changes. It supports contacting the prescriber, but it does not expressly require identity verification. It is therefore less specific than the manual on unknown prescribers.

similarities: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist or provider to check prescription information before filling it [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

Both sources support contacting the prescriber when prescription information needs clarification or review [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual gives a specific exception for missing address, age, or CIN, while the policy update does not discuss those missing patient identifiers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

The provider manual requires a good faith effort to verify prescriber identity when the prescriber is unknown, while the policy update only says to verify directions and consult the prescriber as necessary [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

test_id_41.query_content: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_41.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
It does not include protocols that cover drugs
prescribed to relieve side effects of these therapies or to relieve distressing
symptoms (such as nausea or pain).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2021 Volume 37 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest, encourage, or otherwise require that any practitioner
administered, or dispensed drug be billed to the Program under the pharmacy benefit. Practitioner administered drugs
will continue to be billed as a NYS Medicaid medical benefit.
Questions regarding this policy may be directed to the Medicaid Pharmacy Policy unit at (518)486-3209 or
NYRx@health.ny.gov.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Yes. NYS Medicaid excludes drugs used for weight loss, and the current provider manual also says compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss. This applies when a compound claim is being prepared for payment. The manual also ties the rule to the Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2).
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This is the broader coverage rule for the drug class. It is less specific than the provider manual because it does not focus on compounded claims.

similarities: Both sources treat drugs used for weight loss as excluded from Medicaid coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
differences: The provider manual states the rule in the context of compound claims and excluded indications, while the policy states the broader drug coverage exclusion directly [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
answers.caveats: No overall recency judgment is needed beyond the current provider manual because the newest policy source was unavailable.

answers.headline_summary: Yes. Under current NYS Medicaid pharmacy guidance, drugs used to treat weight loss are excluded. The newer provider manual and the policy update both point to the same exclusion.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss. The manual also says compounded claims must still meet the other reimbursement rules. This is the current provider-manual guidance for compounded products.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss. The policy update is narrower because it speaks to compounded claims rather than all drug coverage. It still gives the same exclusion for weight loss-related use.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid reimbursement excludes drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This is a direct coverage exclusion for the drug use itself. It applies to reimbursable drugs under the pharmacy rules.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Agents used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are excluded. The policy update states the same exclusion, but it is phrased more broadly as an exclusion list item. It does not add a separate operational rule beyond the exclusion.

similarities: Both sources exclude weight loss-related drug use under NYS Medicaid pharmacy guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both sources also tie the exclusion to Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual gives the current, more specific operational rule for compounded claims and direct drug reimbursement, while the policy update is narrower and focuses on the compound restriction in one row and a reminder-style exclusion list in the other [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4; New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].

answers.headline_summary: Yes. New York Medicaid excludes drugs used to treat weight loss, and the current Pharmacy Manual also says compounds may not include excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including weight loss. The retrieved policy evidence is consistent with that exclusion and does not show a separate current exception for weight-loss drugs.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. Drugs used for treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are listed as non-reimbursable.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update is narrower because it speaks to compounded products rather than all drug coverage. It says compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications under New York State Medicaid rules. That includes drugs to treat weight loss or cosmetic purposes.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update says the same operational rule for compounds. It bars compounds made for excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss, under Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2).

similarities: Both sources treat drugs used to treat weight loss as excluded from Medicaid reimbursement or compounding rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both sources point to the same exclusion concept under Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
differences: The Pharmacy Manual states the direct drug-coverage rule, while the policy update focuses on compounded products and is therefore narrower [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

The policy update does not separately discuss all non-compounded weight-loss drugs in the retrieved chunk, so it is less specific than the manual on the general coverage question [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program

• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to

• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.

items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.

contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

answers.headline_summary: Yes. New York Medicaid treats drugs used for weight loss as an excluded, nonreimbursable benefit. The newer provider manual also says compounded drugs may not be made for excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This is a direct coverage exclusion in the pharmacy manual. It applies as a current rule for reimbursable drugs.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update says compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss. This is the closest matching policy statement, but it is narrower because it discusses the compounding context. It confirms that weight-loss drug use remains excluded.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss. The manual also says compounds may not be used to bypass Medicaid criteria. This means compounding cannot be used to work around the exclusion.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update says the same thing for compounds and excluded indications. It also says compounds may not be made to bypass NYRx preferred drug criteria. That makes the policy update broader on compounding controls, while still confirming the weight-loss exclusion.

similarities: Both sources say drugs used to treat weight loss are excluded from Medicaid reimbursement or compounding eligibility [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both sources tie the exclusion to Medicaid compounding rules, not just to standard claim reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual states the broader coverage rule directly and lists weight loss with anorexia and weight gain as nonreimbursable drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines]. The policy update is narrower because it addresses excluded indications in the compounding context [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)

423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD)* 08 No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Outpatient Dispensed Drugs (NCPDP)
As previously communicated in the New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the Ingredient Cost for
340B Drugs article published in the August 2019 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2019/aug19_mu.pdf, for a NYS Medicaid
FFS primary claim only, system editing will compare the ingredient cost submitted (NCPDP field 409-DK)
with the 340B ceiling price for the product, as defined by the Health Resources and Services Administration
(HRSA), to ensure the ingredient cost submitted is the 340B price. The 340B ceiling price refers to the
maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the CE for the purchase of a 340B-covered outpatient drug.
A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid should never be higher than the 340B ceiling price. Pharmacies that
submit a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling price, will be
returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code: “708: Exceeds NY Allowed
Maximum”, and the NCPDP Reject code: "78: Cost Exceeds Maximum”.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B acquisition cost when the claim is properly submitted as a 340B purchased drug claim. NY Medicaid also requires 340B claim-level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts, and outpatient dispensed FFS claims cannot submit an ingredient cost above the 340B ceiling price.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For 340B purchased drugs, the pharmacy manual says reimbursement is the 340B Acquisition Cost. A $10.18 professional dispensing fee applies when the drug meets the covered outpatient drug rules and is not paid at U&C. The claim pays at U&C if that is lower than the ingredient cost plus dispensing fee.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For NYS Medicaid FFS primary outpatient dispensed drug claims, the system compares the submitted ingredient cost with the 340B ceiling price. The submitted ingredient cost must equal the 340B price and may not be higher than that ceiling. If it is higher, the claim is rejected.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NY Medicaid does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. It relies on 340B claim-level identifiers on all 340B drug claims to avoid duplicate discounts and to remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy is narrower on this point and focuses on claim submission edits. It requires the UD modifier for 340B drugs on Medicaid FFS claims, and it also says the NDC must be reported for reimbursement.

similarities: Both sources treat the 340B price as the reimbursement basis for properly identified 340B claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

Both sources are aimed at preventing improper payment on 340B claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].
differences: The pharmacy manual states the payment methodology more broadly, including the dispensing fee and U&C limit, while the policy update focuses only on the ingredient-cost ceiling for FFS outpatient dispensed claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

The pharmacy manual emphasizes claim-level identifiers and rebate-invoice removal, while the policy update names the UD modifier and NDC reporting instead [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD)* 08 No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Outpatient Dispensed Drugs (NCPDP)
As previously communicated in the New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the Ingredient Cost for
340B Drugs article published in the August 2019 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2019/aug19_mu.pdf, for a NYS Medicaid
FFS primary claim only, system editing will compare the ingredient cost submitted (NCPDP field 409-DK)
with the 340B ceiling price for the product, as defined by the Health Resources and Services Administration
(HRSA), to ensure the ingredient cost submitted is the 340B price. The 340B ceiling price refers to the
maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the CE for the purchase of a 340B-covered outpatient drug.
A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid should never be higher than the 340B ceiling price. Pharmacies that
submit a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling price, will be
returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code: “708: Exceeds NY Allowed
Maximum”, and the NCPDP Reject code: "78: Cost Exceeds Maximum”.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.

for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid reimburses 340B drugs at the 340B acquisition cost when the claim is properly identified as a 340B purchased drug claim. Current manual guidance also says the claim pays at the lower of usual and customary if that is lower than ingredient cost plus the dispensing fee, and 340B claims must use claim-level identifiers so Medicaid can exclude them from rebate invoicing.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For 340B purchased drugs, Medicaid reimburses at the 340B acquisition cost. The professional dispensing fee is $10.18 if the drug meets covered outpatient drug requirements and is not paid at usual and customary pricing. The manual also says the claim pays at usual and customary pricing if that is lower than drug ingredient cost plus the dispensing fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For NYS Medicaid fee-for-service primary outpatient dispensed drug claims, the submitted ingredient cost for a 340B drug must not exceed the 340B ceiling price [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11]. Claims above that ceiling are denied. This policy statement is narrower than the manual because it focuses on the ceiling price edit rather than the full reimbursement methodology.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NY Medicaid does not use HRSAs Medicaid Exclusion File. It relies completely on claim-level 340B identifiers for all 340B drug claims. Those identifiers are required at submission so Medicaid can avoid duplicate discounts and remove the claim from rebate invoicing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For NYS Medicaid fee-for-service claims, 340B drugs must be identified with the UD modifier, and the NDC and actual acquisition cost must also be reported [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8]. This policy guidance is more specific because it names the claim fields and modifier, while the manual describes the broader claim-level identification rule.

similarities: Both sources require 340B claims to be identified correctly so Medicaid can process them as 340B claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources tie 340B payment to the 340B price concept rather than a higher billed amount [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].
differences: The manual states the actual reimbursement method, including the 340B acquisition cost and the $10.18 professional dispensing fee rule, while the policy update mainly describes the ceiling price edit and denial outcome [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

The manual says NY Medicaid uses claim-level identifiers and does not use HRSAs Medicaid Exclusion File, while the policy update gives the older UD modifier and field-reporting instructions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost when the claim is properly submitted as a 340B purchased drug. Current Pharmacy Manual guidance also says reimbursement is capped by usual and customary pricing if that is lower.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For eligible 340B purchased drug claims, Medicaid reimburses at 340B Acquisition Cost. Payment is limited to usual and customary pricing if that is lower.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For NYS Medicaid fee-for-service primary claims, the submitted ingredient cost for a 340B drug is compared with the 340B ceiling price. Claims above the ceiling price are returned, so the submitted amount must not exceed that limit.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For 340B purchased drugs, the manual treats actual acquisition cost as the relevant acquisition-cost concept. It means the price paid by the provider without added fees.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update is more operationally specific for fee-for-service claims. It requires the ingredient cost to be submitted as the 340B price by invoice, and it also compares that amount with the 340B ceiling price.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: 340B claims must use claim-level 340B identifiers. The manual says New York Medicaid does not rely on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File to avoid duplicate discounts.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update is narrower on this point. It focuses on the ingredient cost edit for fee-for-service pharmacy claims and does not restate the claim-level identification rule.

similarities: Both sources tie 340B reimbursement to the 340B acquisition or ceiling price framework [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

Both sources make clear that 340B claims have to be submitted correctly for Medicaid to treat them as 340B claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].
differences: The provider manual states the current payment rule as 340B Acquisition Cost with a usual and customary cap, while the policy update adds an edit that rejects fee-for-service claims when the submitted ingredient cost exceeds the 340B ceiling price [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

The provider manual also addresses claim-level 340B identification and duplicate discounts, while the policy update chunk for this row is limited to the ingredient cost validation edit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].
answers.caveats: The policy row is partial because it focuses on fee-for-service primary claim edits, while the provider manual states the broader current reimbursement rule.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD)* 08 No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Outpatient Dispensed Drugs (NCPDP)
As previously communicated in the New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the Ingredient Cost for
340B Drugs article published in the August 2019 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2019/aug19_mu.pdf, for a NYS Medicaid
FFS primary claim only, system editing will compare the ingredient cost submitted (NCPDP field 409-DK)
with the 340B ceiling price for the product, as defined by the Health Resources and Services Administration
(HRSA), to ensure the ingredient cost submitted is the 340B price. The 340B ceiling price refers to the
maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the CE for the purchase of a 340B-covered outpatient drug.
A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid should never be higher than the 340B ceiling price. Pharmacies that
submit a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling price, will be
returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code: “708: Exceeds NY Allowed
Maximum”, and the NCPDP Reject code: "78: Cost Exceeds Maximum”.

**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims

Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency

answers.headline_summary: 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B acquisition cost when the claim is properly identified as a 340B purchased drug. The pharmacy manual also says claims pay at usual and customary if that is lower than ingredient cost plus dispensing fee, but the 340B reimbursement method is 340B acquisition cost.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For 340B purchased drugs, the reimbursement methodology is 340B Acquisition Cost. The manual also says a $10.18 professional dispensing fee applies if the drug qualifies, and claims pay at usual and customary if that is lower than drug ingredient cost plus dispensing fee.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For NYS Medicaid fee-for-service primary claims, the submitted ingredient cost for a 340B drug must not exceed the HRSA 340B ceiling price. Claims above that ceiling are rejected. The policy explains the claim-level price check that supports the reimbursement limit.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NY Medicaid relies on claim-level 340B identifiers to treat a claim as 340B and to keep it out of rebate invoicing. The manual says the Medicaid program does not use the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File for this purpose.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: 340B drug claims submitted in NCPDP D.0 must be properly identified as 340B. The policy points to Submission Clarification Code 20 and a 340B acquisition cost entry in the ingredient cost field. The policy is more specific about the claim fields than the manual chunk.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Prescription drugs billed directly to Medicaid are fee-for-service claims and must meet Medicaid drug-list and utilization requirements. This is broader billing context rather than a 340B-specific payment rule.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For 340B drugs on fee-for-service claims, providers must append the UD modifier, report the NDC, and include actual acquisition cost by invoice in the charges field. The policy is more specific than the manual chunk and gives the 340B reporting fields.

similarities: Both sources tie 340B payment to claim-level identification and control of the ingredient cost submitted for the claim [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

Both sources treat the submitted 340B amount as a ceiling-based payment check rather than an open-ended charge [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].
differences: The manual states the reimbursement method as 340B Acquisition Cost, while the policy update adds a claim edit that rejects ingredient cost above the HRSA 340B ceiling price [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

The manual also includes the usual and customary comparison and the $10.18 dispensing fee rule, while the policy update is narrower and focuses on the 340B ceiling-price validation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].
answers.caveats: The provider manual chunk was the anchor for the reimbursement row, so the broader usual and customary rule is included as related payment context. The policy updates shown here are claim-edit and claim-identification rules, not a separate reimbursement schedule.

test_id_51.query_content: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_51.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid does not pay a bundled amount for home infusion drugs and supplies. They must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, and other insurance must be billed first when it covers the service.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Home infusion drugs and supplies are not covered under a bundled payment. They must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit. The manual also points to the reimbursable drug list for pharmacy-billed coverage.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update covers administered drugs more broadly, including drugs given by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioner’s office. It says those drugs are covered when FDA-approved or otherwise medically accepted, and when the listed clinical and billing guidance is met. It does not describe the home infusion billing pathway as specifically as the provider manual does.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Home infusion drugs may be covered under the pharmacy benefit when they meet pharmacy billing criteria. The drug must be billable in NCPDP format and delivered directly to the place of administration. The manual also requires an enrolled Medicaid dispensing pharmacy.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update is broader and less specific. It says certain administered drugs are reimbursable when the clinical criteria and billing guidance on the practitioner-administered drug policy pages are met. It does not spell out the NCPDP or direct-delivery requirements.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The provider is responsible for determining whether the member has other insurance and whether that insurance covers the service. If it does, the provider must bill the other insurer before Medicaid. Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update matches this rule closely. It says Medicaid is the payer of last resort and that providers must bill commercial insurance or Medicare first when third-party liability applies. It then allows Medicaid only as secondary coverage when appropriate.

similarities: Both sources treat administered drugs as payable under Medicaid when coverage and billing rules are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources require some form of billing review before payment, although the manual is specific to home infusion and the policy update is broader [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources place Medicaid coverage within a rules-based framework rather than a flat bundled payment approach [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says the drugs and supplies are not paid as a bundled service [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines]. The policy update is broader and addresses practitioner-administered drugs in general [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual gives pharmacy-billing requirements such as NCPDP format and direct delivery to the administration site [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines]. The policy update does not include those operational billing details [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
answers.caveats: The policy source is broader than the provider manual and does not specifically address home infusion billing pathways. The provider manual is the best current source for the home infusion billing question.
