policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid does not use a bundled payment for home infusion. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed separately as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, and the provider must bill other insurance first if it applies.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion drugs, supplies, or services. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit. The manual also points to the Medicaid reimbursable drug list for pharmacy benefit billing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy side is broader and is not specific to home infusion. It says drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered for FDA-approved or otherwise medically accepted indications when the stated clinical criteria are met. It also notes that certain drug-class billing guidance applies when the published criteria are met.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The provider is responsible for determining whether the patient has other insurance and whether that insurer covers the service. If it does, the provider must submit the claim to the other insurer before billing Medicaid because Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The billing form also asks for other insurance paid information when applicable.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy statement matches the billing-order rule more generally. It says that if third-party liability applies, providers must bill the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare, and then bill Medicaid as secondary if appropriate. It does not focus on home infusion specifically.

similarities: Both sources treat Medicaid coverage as subject to a defined billing pathway rather than a single bundled payment model [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources require the provider to follow payer and coverage rules before Medicaid payment is used [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says those drugs and supplies are billed as pharmacy or medical benefits, while the policy update is broader and discusses practitioner-office administration of drugs by injection or infusion methods [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual makes the provider responsible for checking other insurance and billing it first, while the policy update states the third-party billing order more generally and is not home-infusion specific [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
answers.caveats: The policy update is only a partial match for the home infusion topic, so the provider manual is the better source for the current home infusion billing rule.

test_id_53.query_content: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_53.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed either as a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, and the provider is responsible for billing the appropriate payer path.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for drugs, supplies, and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit. The manual also points to the pharmacy benefit drug list for reimbursable drugs.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update gives a broader coverage rule for administered drugs. It says drugs given by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered when the FDA-approved or compendia-supported indication standard is met for a medically necessary covered service. It does not state the home infusion billing pathway.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Home infusion drugs may be covered under the pharmacy benefit only if they meet specific billing criteria. The drug must be billable in NCPDP format, delivered directly to the place of administration, not included in a facility rate, and dispensed by a Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy. The manual also requires pharmacist counseling and record retention.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update is broader and less specific. It covers practitioner-administered drugs when the indication is FDA approved or supported by recognized compendia, and it says an Institutional Review Board protocol is required if there is no recognized indication. It does not describe the pharmacy billing criteria for home infusion drugs.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The provider is responsible for determining whether the member has other insurance and whether that insurance covers the service. If it does, the provider must bill that carrier before Medicaid because Medicaid is the payer of last resort. This applies before Medicaid billing for the claim.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update matches the same coordination of benefits rule. It states that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and that providers must bill the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare, before billing Medicaid. The policy statement is general rather than home-infusion-specific.

similarities: Both sources treat infusion-related drugs as part of Medicaid coverage, but the policy source states the coverage rule more generally for administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources require another payer to be billed first when other insurance applies [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual gives the current home infusion billing pathway, while the policy update gives only a broader administered-drug coverage standard and does not describe the home infusion billing split [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual names concrete pharmacy-benefit requirements such as NCPDP billing and Medicaid-enrolled dispensing, while the policy update focuses on FDA-approved or compendia-supported indications and is less specific about home infusion billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid does not pay a bundled home-infusion rate. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, and the provider must bill other insurance first when it applies.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion drugs, supplies, and services. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. The manual also points to the Medicaid reimbursable drug list for pharmacy benefit coverage.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update covers practitioner-administered drugs for medically necessary Medicaid-covered services when they are FDA approved or supported by accepted compendia. It is broader than home infusion and does not give the same billing-pathway rule. Drugs are not covered for investigational or experimental use.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Home infusion drugs may be covered through either the medical benefit or the pharmacy benefit. To qualify for the pharmacy benefit, the drug must meet criteria such as NCPDP billing, direct delivery to the place of administration, and not being included in a facility rate. The manual also says the drug must come from a Medicaid-enrolled dispensing pharmacy.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update says practitioner-administered drugs are covered when medically necessary and supported by approved indications or compendia. It is less specific than the manual because it does not list the pharmacy-benefit billing criteria for home infusion. It also says an approved Institutional Review Board protocol is needed when there is no recognized indication.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The provider is responsible for determining whether other insurance exists and whether it covers the service. If the service is covered or coverage is unknown, the provider must bill the other insurer before Medicaid. Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update says providers and pharmacies must actively seek third-party liability coverage and bill the correct party. It also says pharmacies must bill timely and appropriately to the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. This is consistent with the manual’s billing-responsibility rule.

similarities: Both sources say home infusion-related drugs can be covered only when they meet Medicaid coverage rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources tie coverage to medical necessity and recognized coverage standards rather than open-ended payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources place billing responsibility on the provider or pharmacy rather than on Medicaid by default [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment, while the policy update is broader and addresses practitioner-administered drugs generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual gives concrete pharmacy-benefit criteria such as NCPDP billing and direct delivery, while the policy update instead discusses FDA approval, compendia support, and Institutional Review Board protocols [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The billing guidelines specifically say the provider must bill other insurance first when coverage exists or is unknown, while the policy update frames the point more generally as a pharmacy and provider obligation to seek third-party liability [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

test_id_55.query_content: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_55.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid does not pay home infusion drugs and supplies as one bundled package. They must be billed separately as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit. This is the current billing guidance in the Pharmacy Manual.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy covers drugs administered by injection or infusion in the practitioner setting when coverage criteria are met. It is broader and does not give the same home-infusion billing rule. It also states that investigational or experimental use is not covered [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The provider must determine whether the member has other insurance and whether that insurance covers the service. If it does, or if coverage is unknown, the provider must bill the other insurer before Medicaid. Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers are responsible for obtaining correct billing information and billing the appropriate party first. The policy is broader, but it matches the same basic first-bill responsibility. It says pharmacies must bill the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid as appropriate [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

similarities: Both sources treat infusion-related drugs as potentially reimbursable when coverage rules are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources place billing responsibility on the provider or pharmacy to follow the correct payer sequence when another payer is involved [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual gives the specific home infusion billing pathway, while the policy is broader and only describes practitioner-administered drug coverage generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual says to bill separately as a pharmacy or medical benefit, while the policy does not address that home infusion split [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

policy.text:
Providers will be reimbursed $17.85 for the administration of the vaccine.
Article 28 clinics that partner with LCHDs for vaccine administration should follow the billing guidelines listed
above. Similarly, private practitioners [i.e., physicians, nurse practitioners (NPs), and midwives (MWs)] may
bill a professional claim for vaccine administration using the billing guidelines listed above.
July 2022 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 14

School Based Health Centers (SBHCs) would follow the ordered ambulatory billing instructions above and
append the “HA” modifier to both the CPT code for the vaccine administered (in addition to the appropriate
“FB” or “SL” modifier) and CPT code for vaccine administration.
Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs), including SBHCs designated as FQHCs (SBHC/FQHC), may
submit a Prospective Payment System (PPS) threshold clinic claim if the vaccine is administered as part of
an encounter in which a significant procedure and/or medical visit accompanies the vaccination.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 2

Policy and Billing
For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering prescriber
in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug
Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology. For the administration, the claim must be submitted using
procedure code “96372” and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed
the administration fee of $13.36.
Questions and Additional Information:
• Pharmacists should refer to the NYSED “Pharmacists” web page, located at: https://www.op.
nysed.gov/pharmacist, for additional information.
• NYRx claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
• NYRx Pharmacy coverage and policy questions should be directed to the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy
Policy Unit by telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
February 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 4

Reminder: Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner
of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued
naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found in the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) Non-Patient Specific Prescription for Naloxone with Pharmacy Dispensing
Protocol document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention
/docs/naloxone_standing_order_pharmacies.pdf.
To comply with NYS laws, regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, a pharmacy must submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
When dispensing naloxone OTC
via a standing order, all the following must apply:
• a NYS Medicaid member had specifically requested the item on the date of service (DOS); or
• a pharmacist-initiated dispensing per a determination of need/risk on DOS for a NYS Medicaid
member; and
• a pharmacy submits one course of therapy (two doses) with no refills; and
• the drug item(s) are dispensed according to:
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) guidelines;
NYS laws, rules, and regulations; and
NYS Medicaid Policy.
Billing a Standing Order:
1. Enter a value of "5" in the Prescription Origin Code field 419-DJ to indicate pharmacy dispensing;
2.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.

TCN 082609865432123 was paid on
October 1, 2008. Later, the provider became aware that the patient was covered by other insurance. The other
insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Naloxone is reimbursed by NYS Medicaid when it is prescribed for a Medicaid member or dispensed under the State-issued standing order. Pharmacy-billed naloxone claims are paid as NDC-based medication claims, and OTC standing-order dispensing is limited to one course of therapy with no refills.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The retrieved provider manual chunk does not give naloxone-specific reimbursement rules. It only says that compounds are reimbursed only when ingredients are on the Medicaid reimbursable drug list and meet the other compound limits. That makes it only an indirect anchor for naloxone reimbursement.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid covers naloxone OTC when it is prescribed for a Medicaid member or dispensed under the State-issued standing order. The pharmacy must submit a claim to dispense it. The policy statement is more specific than the provider manual on this topic.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: When both the drug and administration are billed, the claims must be submitted separately. The drug claim uses the ordering prescriber's National Provider Identifier in the Prescriber ID field, and the medication is reimbursed when billed via National Drug Code using reimbursement methodology. The manual also says the administration claim is billed separately with procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy's National Provider Identifier.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For naloxone-related billing, the drug claim uses the ordering prescribers National Provider Identifier in the Prescriber ID field and is reimbursed when billed via National Drug Code using reimbursement methodology. The administration claim uses procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy National Provider Identifier, and the pharmacy receives a set administration fee of $13.36.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The retrieved provider manual chunk does not address naloxone standing orders. It only states that reimbursement is available for multiple source drugs on the Medicaid reimbursable drug list. That makes it a weak match for the standing-order limit question.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: When dispensing naloxone OTC under a standing order, the member must request it or the pharmacist must determine need or risk on the date of service. The pharmacy may submit only one course of therapy, which is two doses, with no refills. The policy is much more specific than the provider manual on this point.

similarities: Both sources support reimbursement through pharmacy billing rather than informal dispensing.
Both sources use a claim-based reimbursement pathway for the medication portion, including National Drug Code billing for the drug claim.
differences: The provider manual is general and does not name naloxone in the retrieved chunks, while the policy update gives naloxone-specific coverage and standing-order rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

The policy update adds a standing-order limit of one course of therapy with no refills, which is not stated in the provider manual chunk [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
answers.caveats: The provider manual chunks retrieved here are general pharmacy rules and do not contain a naloxone-specific reimbursement provision.

policy.text:
Providers will be reimbursed $17.85 for the administration of the vaccine.
Article 28 clinics that partner with LCHDs for vaccine administration should follow the billing guidelines listed
above. Similarly, private practitioners [i.e., physicians, nurse practitioners (NPs), and midwives (MWs)] may
bill a professional claim for vaccine administration using the billing guidelines listed above.
July 2022 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 14

School Based Health Centers (SBHCs) would follow the ordered ambulatory billing instructions above and
append the “HA” modifier to both the CPT code for the vaccine administered (in addition to the appropriate
“FB” or “SL” modifier) and CPT code for vaccine administration.
Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs), including SBHCs designated as FQHCs (SBHC/FQHC), may
submit a Prospective Payment System (PPS) threshold clinic claim if the vaccine is administered as part of
an encounter in which a significant procedure and/or medical visit accompanies the vaccination.

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 2

Policy and Billing
For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering prescriber
in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug
Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology. For the administration, the claim must be submitted using
procedure code “96372” and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed
the administration fee of $13.36.
Questions and Additional Information:
• Pharmacists should refer to the NYSED “Pharmacists” web page, located at: https://www.op.
nysed.gov/pharmacist, for additional information.
• NYRx claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
• NYRx Pharmacy coverage and policy questions should be directed to the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy
Policy Unit by telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.

policy.text:
February 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 4

Reminder: Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner
of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued
naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found in the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) Non-Patient Specific Prescription for Naloxone with Pharmacy Dispensing
Protocol document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention
/docs/naloxone_standing_order_pharmacies.pdf.
To comply with NYS laws, regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, a pharmacy must submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
When dispensing naloxone OTC
via a standing order, all the following must apply:
• a NYS Medicaid member had specifically requested the item on the date of service (DOS); or
• a pharmacist-initiated dispensing per a determination of need/risk on DOS for a NYS Medicaid
member; and
• a pharmacy submits one course of therapy (two doses) with no refills; and
• the drug item(s) are dispensed according to:
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) guidelines;
NYS laws, rules, and regulations; and
NYS Medicaid Policy.
Billing a Standing Order:
1. Enter a value of "5" in the Prescription Origin Code field 419-DJ to indicate pharmacy dispensing;
2.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.

provider_manual.text:
TCN 082609865432123 was paid on
October 1, 2008. Later, the provider became aware that the patient was covered by other insurance. The other
insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.

answers.headline_summary: Naloxone is reimbursed by NYS Medicaid when it is prescribed to a Medicaid member or dispensed under the State-issued naloxone standing order. The clearest billing detail is that OTC naloxone is submitted as a claim, and medication reimbursement uses NDC and the reimbursement methodology; if administration is billed separately, it uses procedure code 96372 and a separate claim.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The provider manual does not give naloxone-specific reimbursement rules in the retrieved chunk. It only states that compound ingredients are reimbursed if they are on the Medicaid reimbursable-drugs list and meet the other compounding rules. This makes the manual guidance broader than the naloxone policy.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NY Medicaid covers over-the-counter naloxone when it is prescribed to a Medicaid member or dispensed under the State-issued naloxone standing order. The pharmacy must submit a claim and dispense it under a fiscal order or standing order. This is the closest direct policy statement on naloxone coverage.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: When billing both a drug and its administration, the claims must be submitted separately. The drug is reimbursed when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology. The administration is billed with procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For naloxone, the drug claim uses the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field and is reimbursed when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology. The administration claim uses procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The policy also states a current administration fee of $13.36.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The provider manual does not describe naloxone standing-order limits in the retrieved chunk. It instead says multiple source drugs are reimbursable only if they are on the Medicaid reimbursable-drugs list. It also gives general brand-name certification rules, so it is only loosely related to standing-order dispensing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: When dispensing naloxone OTC under a standing order, the pharmacy must meet specific conditions. The member must request the item on the date of service, or the pharmacist must determine need or risk on that date. The pharmacy must dispense one course of therapy with no refills and follow the standing-order billing steps.

similarities: Both sources support reimbursement for covered drug claims, but the manual states the rule only generally while the policy gives naloxone-specific coverage details [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

Both sources require separate billing when a medication administration is involved, and both use procedure code 96372 for the administration claim [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is broader and does not mention naloxone specifically in the retrieved chunks, while the policy update directly addresses OTC naloxone coverage and standing-order dispensing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

The policy update adds naloxone-specific operational details, including the standing-order limits and the $13.36 administration fee, while the provider manual only gives the general separate-claims billing method [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
answers.caveats: The provider manual chunks retrieved here are general and do not contain a naloxone-specific reimbursement rule, so the comparison relies on the policy updates for the direct naloxone guidance.
